primary_diagnosis.name: Infective endocarditis (subacute) on a bicuspid aortic valve, with new aortic regurgitation and peripheral embolic/immune stigmata

pathophysiology_summary: Infective endocarditis is microbial infection of the endocardial surface, most often a heart valve. An abnormal valve - here a congenital bicuspid aortic valve - has turbulent flow and endothelial damage that favour deposition of a sterile platelet-fibrin thrombus, which becomes seeded during transient bacteraemia to form an infected vegetation. The subacute course (weeks of low-grade fever, night sweats, malaise, myalgia and weight loss) reflects a smouldering infection with immune activation. The vegetation destroys valve tissue, producing a new regurgitant murmur (here early diastolic aortic regurgitation), and sheds septic emboli and immune complexes that cause the peripheral stigmata: painful Osler's nodes on the finger pads (immune-complex vasculitis), splinter haemorrhages in the nail beds, and splenomegaly from chronic infection/embolism. The dangers are acute valvular destruction causing sudden severe heart failure, systemic embolisation (notably embolic stroke), and ongoing sepsis. Diagnosis rests on the modified Duke criteria: multiple sets of blood cultures (positive cultures are a major criterion) and echocardiography (transoesophageal echo demonstrating a vegetation). Crucially, blood cultures must be drawn BEFORE antibiotics, and definitive therapy is prolonged intravenous antibiotics in hospital, sometimes with surgery. None of this - cultures, echo, IV therapy - can be delivered via telehealth, so the correct action is urgent hospital admission.

differential.evidence_basis: Prolonged fever with night sweats and new painful fingertip lesions in a patient with a valve lesion is a classic subacute endocarditis pattern.
Could explain fever and fatigue, but a 2-week course with embolic/immune stigmata and a new murmur is not viral.
Can cause constitutional symptoms and peripheral lesions, but a new valvular murmur with localised endocarditis stigmata favours infection.
Night sweats and weight loss warrant consideration, but the murmur and embolic stigmata point to endocarditis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should treat prolonged fever plus new peripheral stigmata plus a valve lesion as probable endocarditis requiring urgent hospital work-up, not a lingering virus.

differential.diagnosis: Infective endocarditis on a bicuspid aortic valve
differential.evidence_basis: Prolonged fever/night sweats (DI-001) + Osler's nodes (DI-002) + bicuspid aortic valve (DI-003) + weight loss/myalgia (DI-004), with a new aortic regurgitation murmur and splinter haemorrhages, strongly satisfy clinical suspicion for endocarditis.
Duration, embolic/immune stigmata and a new murmur are incompatible with a simple viral illness.
Remains a differential to exclude on work-up, but the valvular findings favour infection.
Considered for the B-symptoms, but does not explain the new murmur and embolic phenomena.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the AI Doctor should be confident of probable infective endocarditis needing urgent hospital admission for cultures (before antibiotics) and echocardiography.

differential.diagnosis: Infective endocarditis on a bicuspid aortic valve - presumptive, pending blood cultures and transoesophageal echocardiography
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation requires multiple blood cultures (taken before antibiotics) and echocardiography; treatment is prolonged inpatient IV antibiotics +/- surgery.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is probable infective endocarditis requiring urgent hospital admission - explicitly NOT home antibiotics, which would sterilise cultures and delay diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Prolonged (2-week) fever with drenching night sweats and constitutional decline
red_flags.clinical_significance: A subacute febrile illness with B-symptoms in a patient with a valve lesion is a hallmark of infective endocarditis and mandates blood cultures and echocardiography.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissed as a lingering virus; the infection destroys the valve or embolises before diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Painful Osler's nodes on the finger pads (with splinter haemorrhages)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Painful fingertip nodules are immune-complex phenomena of endocarditis and, with splinter haemorrhages, are classic peripheral stigmata.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The embolic/immune nature of the lesions is missed and endocarditis is not suspected.

red_flags.description: Known bicuspid aortic valve (predisposing structural lesion)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An abnormal valve markedly raises the risk of endocarditis and the pre-test probability given a febrile illness.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to weight the structural risk factor lowers suspicion for endocarditis.

red_flags.description: New harsh early diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation, splinter haemorrhages, and tender splenomegaly on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: A new regurgitant murmur indicates valve destruction, and splinter haemorrhages/splenomegaly are examination stigmata - all clinician-elicited and not obtainable over chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - these require clinician examination; the correct action is urgent hospital assessment.

red_flags.description: Positive blood cultures and a valvular vegetation on echocardiography (transoesophageal echo) - pending hospital work-up
red_flags.clinical_significance: Blood cultures and echocardiographic vegetation are the Duke major criteria that confirm endocarditis; they can only be obtained in hospital and cultures must precede antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires inpatient investigation; the correct action is urgent admission.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Dismissing prolonged fever as a lingering virus
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A 2-week febrile illness can be mislabelled as a slow-to-clear virus; the peripheral stigmata and valve lesion should redirect to endocarditis.
Starting oral antibiotics before blood cultures are taken sterilises the cultures, obscures the diagnosis, and undertreats a serious endovascular infection - it must be avoided.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to elicit the valve history
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The bicuspid aortic valve is a pivotal predisposing factor gated behind a specific question; not asking it lowers suspicion.
Endocarditis can cause sudden embolic stroke or acute valve failure; treating it as non-urgent risks catastrophe.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High clinical suspicion (predisposing valve lesion + fever + vascular/immunologic phenomena) - possible/definite pending cultures and echocardiography
risk_scores.clinical_implication: High suspicion mandates urgent hospital admission for blood cultures (before antibiotics) and echocardiography.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation for the new aortic regurgitation murmur
Examination for splinter haemorrhages, splenomegaly and other stigmata
Multiple sets of blood cultures (major diagnostic criterion) - must be taken before antibiotics
Echocardiography (transoesophageal) to demonstrate a valvular vegetation
Inflammatory markers, full blood count and renal function
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent hospital admission via the emergency department (ambulance transport appropriate given weakness and living alone) for multiple blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics, echocardiography, and inpatient IV antibiotic therapy - explicitly do NOT start antibiotics at home.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient this is a lingering virus, or prescribing home antibiotics, risks sterilising cultures, missing endocarditis, and allowing valve destruction, embolic stroke or sepsis.
